content.text: Video generation with diffusion models extends image diffusion to four dimensions: height, width, and time. The core challenge is temporal consistency — frames must be spatially coherent with each other, not just individually plausible. This requires the model to learn long-range temporal dependencies across frames, which naive frame-by-frame generation fails to do. The key architectural innovation is factorized space-time attention: first attend spatially within each frame, then attend temporally across frames.
Why Video Is Harder Than Images
An image diffusion model denoises a 3D tensor
. A video adds a temporal axis, making the input
is the number of frames. Processing this naively with 3D convolutions or full 4D attention is computationally prohibitive — full attention across all spatial and temporal positions scales as
. For a 16-frame, 64×64 clip, that is ~67M attention pairs per head. For 256×256, it is ~4 billion.

Beyond compute, temporal consistency introduces semantic constraints: an object moving behind an occluder must re-appear correctly, lighting must evolve plausibly, and motion must be physically coherent. These constraints are non-local in time, requiring the model to attend across all frames rather than just adjacent ones.

The Video Diffusion Models paper (Ho et al., 2022) introduces factorized attention: each block first applies spatial attention (within each frame independently) then temporal attention (across frames at each spatial position). This reduces the complexity from
— a dramatic saving while preserving the ability to model both kinds of dependency.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class SpatialAttention(nn.Module):
    """Standard self-attention over spatial positions, independent per frame."""

    def __init__(self, dim: int, num_heads: int = 8):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(dim)
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(dim, num_heads, batch_first=True)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # x: [B, T, H*W, C]
        B, T, N, C = x.shape
        # Merge batch and time: attend spatially within each frame
        x_flat = x.view(B * T, N, C)
        h = self.norm(x_flat)
        h, _ = self.attn(h, h, h)
        return x + h.view(B, T, N, C)


class TemporalAttention(nn.Module):
    """Self-attention over the time axis at each spatial position."""

    def __init__(self, dim: int, num_heads: int = 8):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(dim)
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(dim, num_heads, batch_first=True)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # x: [B, T, N, C]  where N = H*W
        B, T, N, C = x.shape
        # Merge batch and spatial: attend temporally at each location
        x_t = x.permute(0, 2, 1, 3).reshape(B * N, T, C)   # [B*N, T, C]
        h = self.norm(x_t)
        h, _ = self.attn(h, h, h)
        h = h.view(B, N, T, C).permute(0, 2, 1, 3)           # [B, T, N, C]
        return x + h


class SpaceTimeBlock(nn.Module):
    """Factorized spatial-then-temporal transformer block."""

    def __init__(self, dim: int, num_heads: int = 8):
        super().__init__()
        self.spatial_attn = SpatialAttention(dim, num_heads)
        self.temporal_attn = TemporalAttention(dim, num_heads)
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(dim)
        self.ffn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(dim, dim * 4),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(dim * 4, dim),
        )

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # x: [B, T, H*W, C]
        x = self.spatial_attn(x)   # within each frame
        x = self.temporal_attn(x)  # across frames
        x = x + self.ffn(self.norm(x))
        return x

The video U-Net adapts the image diffusion U-Net by inserting temporal attention after each spatial attention block. 2D spatial convolutions are either kept as-is (treating T as a batch dimension) or replaced with pseudo-3D convolutions (a 2D conv followed by a 1D temporal conv) to capture local motion.

class Pseudo3DConv(nn.Module):
    """Spatial 2D conv + temporal 1D conv — captures local motion cheaply."""

    def __init__(self, in_ch: int, out_ch: int, spatial_k: int = 3, temporal_k: int = 3):
        super().__init__()
        self.spatial = nn.Conv2d(in_ch, out_ch, spatial_k, padding=spatial_k // 2)
        self.temporal = nn.Conv1d(out_ch, out_ch, temporal_k, padding=temporal_k // 2)
        self.norm = nn.GroupNorm(min(8, out_ch), out_ch)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # x: [B, C, T, H, W]
        B, C, T, H, W = x.shape
        # Spatial pass: treat T as batch
        x_s = x.permute(0, 2, 1, 3, 4).reshape(B * T, C, H, W)  # [B*T, C, H, W]
        x_s = self.spatial(x_s)                                    # [B*T, C', H, W]
        Co = x_s.shape[1]
        x_s = x_s.view(B, T, Co, H, W).permute(0, 2, 1, 3, 4)   # [B, C', T, H, W]
        # Temporal pass: treat spatial as batch
        x_t = x_s.permute(0, 3, 4, 1, 2).reshape(B * H * W, Co, T)  # [B*H*W, C', T]
        x_t = self.temporal(x_t)                                       # [B*H*W, C', T]
        x_t = x_t.view(B, H, W, Co, T).permute(0, 3, 4, 1, 2)       # [B, C', T, H, W]
        return F.silu(self.norm(x_t.reshape(B * T, Co, H, W))).view(B, Co, T, H, W)


class VideoResBlock(nn.Module):
    """ResBlock for video: pseudo-3D conv + AdaGN time conditioning."""

    def __init__(self, in_ch: int, out_ch: int, time_emb_dim: int = 256):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = Pseudo3DConv(in_ch, out_ch)
        self.conv2 = Pseudo3DConv(out_ch, out_ch)
        self.time_proj = nn.Linear(time_emb_dim, out_ch * 2)  # scale + shift
        self.skip = nn.Conv2d(in_ch, out_ch, 1) if in_ch != out_ch else nn.Identity()
        self.norm1 = nn.GroupNorm(min(8, in_ch), in_ch)
        self.norm2 = nn.GroupNorm(min(8, out_ch), out_ch)

    def forward(
        self,
        x: torch.Tensor,        # [B, C, T, H, W]
        t_emb: torch.Tensor,    # [B, time_emb_dim]
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, C, T, H, W = x.shape
        # Spatial norms apply per-frame: reshape
        h = x.permute(0, 2, 1, 3, 4).reshape(B * T, C, H, W)
        h = F.silu(self.norm1(h)).view(B, T, C, H, W).permute(0, 2, 1, 3, 4)
        h = self.conv1(h)
        # AdaGN conditioning
        scale, shift = self.time_proj(t_emb).chunk(2, dim=-1)  # [B, out_ch] each
        scale = scale.view(B, -1, 1, 1, 1)
        shift = shift.view(B, -1, 1, 1, 1)
        Co = h.shape[1]
        h_flat = h.permute(0, 2, 1, 3, 4).reshape(B * T, Co, H, W)
        h_normed = self.norm2(h_flat).view(B, T, Co, H, W).permute(0, 2, 1, 3, 4)
        h = h_normed * (1 + scale) + shift
        h = F.silu(h)
        h = self.conv2(h)
        # Skip connection
        skip = x.permute(0, 2, 1, 3, 4).reshape(B * T, C, H, W)
        skip = self.skip(skip).view(B, T, -1, H, W).permute(0, 2, 1, 3, 4)
        return h + skip
Video diffusion training uses the same
-prediction objective as image DDPM, but the noise is added jointly to all frames with the same timestep
. The forward process corrupts each frame independently (i.i.d. noise per frame), while the reverse model must learn to denoise them jointly to achieve temporal coherence.

def video_diffusion_loss(
    model,
    video: torch.Tensor,      # [B, C, T, H, W] in [-1, 1]
    alphas_bar: torch.Tensor, # [T_steps]
    t_emb_fn,                 # timestep -> embedding
    text_emb: torch.Tensor,   # [B, L, D] text conditioning
    device: torch.device,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    B, C, T, H, W = video.shape
    # Sample a single diffusion timestep per clip
    t = torch.randint(0, len(alphas_bar), (B,), device=device)
    ab = alphas_bar[t].view(B, 1, 1, 1, 1)  # broadcast over [C, T, H, W]

    # Add i.i.d. Gaussian noise to every frame
    eps = torch.randn_like(video)
    x_t = ab.sqrt() * video + (1 - ab).sqrt() * eps

    # Predict noise (model sees all frames jointly)
    t_emb = t_emb_fn(t)                        # [B, emb_dim]
    eps_pred = model(x_t, t_emb, text_emb)     # [B, C, T, H, W]
    return F.mse_loss(eps_pred, eps)

Text-to-video models (Make-A-Video, CogVideoX) add text conditioning via cross-attention from frame features to text embeddings, identically to text-to-image. Image-to-video models (Stable Video Diffusion) additionally condition on a single reference frame — its CLIP or VAE encoding is injected via cross-attention or channel concatenation to anchor appearance across all generated frames.

class ImageConditionedVideoModel(nn.Module):
    """
    Image-to-video: generate T frames conditioned on a reference image.
    The reference image feature is cross-attended by every frame.
    """

    def __init__(self, dim: int = 512, num_heads: int = 8, num_frames: int = 16):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_frames = num_frames
        # Encode reference image into a context embedding
        self.img_encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(3, dim, 4, stride=4),   # rough patchify
            nn.Flatten(2),                     # [B, dim, N_patches]
        )
        # Cross-attention: each frame attends to reference image patches
        self.cross_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(dim, num_heads, batch_first=True)
        self.norm_q = nn.LayerNorm(dim)
        self.norm_kv = nn.LayerNorm(dim)

    def forward(
        self,
        x: torch.Tensor,        # [B, C, T, H, W] noisy video
        ref_img: torch.Tensor,  # [B, 3, H, W] reference frame
        t_emb: torch.Tensor,    # [B, dim]
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, C, T, H, W = x.shape
        # Encode reference image: [B, dim, N] → [B, N, dim]
        ref_feat = self.img_encoder(ref_img).permute(0, 2, 1)  # [B, N_patches, dim]

        # For each frame, cross-attend to reference features
        # Flatten spatial: [B, T, H*W, C] reshape for cross-attn
        x_flat = x.permute(0, 2, 3, 4, 1).reshape(B * T, H * W, C)
        ref_expanded = ref_feat.unsqueeze(1).expand(-1, T, -1, -1).reshape(B * T, -1, C)

        q = self.norm_q(x_flat)
        kv = self.norm_kv(ref_expanded)
        attended, _ = self.cross_attn(q, kv, kv)
        x_flat = x_flat + attended   # residual

        return x_flat.view(B, T, H, W, C).permute(0, 4, 1, 2, 3)  # [B, C, T, H, W]
A base video diffusion model generates a fixed number of frames (e.g., 16). To produce longer videos, the standard approach is autoregressive extension: the last

frames from the previous clip are treated as conditioning context for the next clip. The model is trained with these context frames provided as clean frames (not noised), concatenated channel-wise with the noisy target frames.

def autoregressive_video_extension(
    model,
    initial_frames: torch.Tensor,  # [B, C, k, H, W] conditioning frames (clean)
    text_emb: torch.Tensor,
    alphas_bar: torch.Tensor,
    betas: torch.Tensor,
    n_clips: int = 4,
    T_frames: int = 16,            # frames to generate per clip
    k_context: int = 4,            # overlap frames from previous clip
    ddim_steps: int = 50,
    device: torch.device = torch.device('cpu'),
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Extend a video by generating n_clips clips, each conditioned on the
    last k_context frames of the previous clip.
    """
    all_frames = [initial_frames]
    context = initial_frames[:, :, -k_context:]  # [B, C, k, H, W]

    for clip_idx in range(n_clips):
        B, C, _, H, W = context.shape
        # Start from pure noise for T_frames new frames
        x = torch.randn(B, C, T_frames, H, W, device=device)

        # DDIM-style sampling
        timesteps = torch.linspace(len(alphas_bar) - 1, 0, ddim_steps, dtype=torch.long)
        for t_idx, t in enumerate(timesteps):
            t_batch = torch.full((B,), t, device=device, dtype=torch.long)
            ab_t = alphas_bar[t]

            # Concatenate context (clean) and noisy target along time axis
            # Model receives [k_context clean | T_frames noisy] frames
            model_input = torch.cat([context, x], dim=2)  # [B, C, k+T, H, W]
            eps_pred = model(model_input, t_batch, text_emb)
            # Only use prediction for the T_frames noisy frames
            eps_pred = eps_pred[:, :, k_context:]  # [B, C, T, H, W]

            # DDIM update for x
            x0_pred = (x - (1 - ab_t).sqrt() * eps_pred) / ab_t.sqrt()
            x0_pred = x0_pred.clamp(-1, 1)
            if t_idx < len(timesteps) - 1:
                ab_prev = alphas_bar[timesteps[t_idx + 1]]
                x = ab_prev.sqrt() * x0_pred + (1 - ab_prev).sqrt() * eps_pred
            else:
                x = x0_pred

        all_frames.append(x)
        context = x[:, :, -k_context:]  # update context for next clip

    return torch.cat(all_frames, dim=2)  # [B, C, k + n_clips*T, H, W]
Generating in pixel space at high resolution is impractical. Most modern models (SVD, CogVideoX) use a video VAE that encodes
using spatial factor f=8 and temporal factor t=4.
SVD conditions on a scalar "motion bucket ID" that controls how much motion to generate — low values produce nearly-static outputs, high values produce large motion.
For autoregressive streaming video, replacing full temporal attention with causal (lower-triangular mask) attention enables real-time generation where future frames do not attend to past.

Video models require large-scale video-text datasets (WebVid, HD-VILA, Panda-70M). Training from scratch is extremely expensive (~$1M+); most work fine-tunes or adapts pre-trained image diffusion models by inflating 2D convolutions to pseudo-3D.

Image diffusion generates a single canvas; video diffusion generates a stack of canvases that must cohere as if painted in sequence. Factorized attention solves this by first ensuring each canvas is internally consistent (spatial attention), then ensuring the stack tells a coherent story (temporal attention). Pseudo-3D convolutions add cheap local motion awareness, while autoregressive extension stitches clips by anchoring each new clip to the clean end of the previous one.
Factorized space-time attention (spatial within frames, temporal across frames) reduces complexity from O(T²H²W²) to O(T·H²W² + T²·HW), making video diffusion tractable.
Pseudo-3D convolutions (2D spatial + 1D temporal) capture local motion at low cost and allow inflating image model weights to video models.
The forward process adds i.i.d. noise to all frames at the same timestep; the model must jointly denoise them, enforcing temporal consistency through shared attention.
Autoregressive extension generates long videos by conditioning each new clip on the final k clean frames of the previous clip.
Modern video diffusion models (SVD, CogVideoX) operate in a video latent space with joint spatial (f=8) and temporal (t=4) compression to make high-resolution generation feasible.
